title: Canonical Positioning
content:
Joz designs AI-native intelligence and execution platforms that combine trusted context, specialist agents, secure tool use, deterministic policy controls, governed execution, and verifiable outcomes.

The objective is not to create another chatbot. The objective is to build systems that understand intent, retrieve trusted context, reason about possible actions, execute safely, and verify results.

title: Core Agentic Architecture
content:
User Intent → API Layer → Orchestrator → Specialist Agents → Tools → Risk and Policy → Execution → Verification

The API layer receives authenticated requests.
The orchestrator manages workflow state, routing, loops, branching, and retries.
Agents interpret goals and decide which tools are required.
Tools expose APIs, databases, models, and external systems.
Risk and policy services validate permissions, limits, safety, and approvals.
Execution services perform approved actions.
Verification services confirm the final result using authoritative systems.

title: Agent Definition
content:
Agent = LLM + instructions + tools + memory + reasoning loop.

An agent is an AI worker with a defined responsibility. It interprets a goal, selects approved tools, reads relevant context, updates workflow state, and iterates until the task is complete or requires human input.

title: Orchestration
content:
Agent orchestration coordinates agents, tools, memory, and workflow state.

LangGraph is suitable for reasoning graphs, branching, loops, shared state, and multi-agent coordination.
Temporal is suitable for durable execution, retries, approvals, crash recovery, and long-running workflows.
Custom state machines can be used when requirements are simple or highly specialized.

title: Single-Agent and Multi-Agent
content:
Start with one orchestrator agent. Use multiple agents only when responsibilities are clearly separable.

Typical specialists:
Research Agent
Portfolio Agent
Risk Agent
Execution Agent
Verification Agent
Supervisor Agent

Agents should communicate through typed shared state rather than unrestricted free-form conversations.

title: Tooling
content:
Tools are functions, APIs, services, databases, models, or external systems that agents can call.

Examples:
get_portfolio()
get_market_data()
search_documents()
calculate_risk()
create_order()
verify_transaction()

A tool executes a capability. An agent decides which capability is needed to achieve a goal.

title: MCP
content:
MCP means Model Context Protocol.

MCP standardizes how AI clients discover and use tools.

Agent = thinks and decides.
MCP = connects models to tools.
Tool = performs a capability.

MCP is not an agent and does not replace orchestration, policy, execution, or verification.

title: FastAPI
content:
FastAPI is a Python framework used to build backend APIs and services.

In a web application:
Frontend → FastAPI → Orchestrator → Agents → Tools → Response

FastAPI receives requests, authenticates users, validates input, calls workflows or services, and returns structured responses. It is the API entry point, not the AI reasoning layer.

title: Risk Architecture
content:
Risk is evaluated before execution.

Inputs:
Structured action proposal
Live portfolio and balances
Market data
Account limits
Liquidity and volatility data
Collateral and margin state

Outputs:
Approve
Reject
Require human approval
Request a safer alternative

Checks include permissions, balance, collateral, leverage, exposure, concentration, slippage, liquidity, liquidation risk, and transaction limits.

title: Verification Architecture
content:
Verification happens after execution.

Inputs:
Expected result
Order and fill events
Ledger state
Updated balances and positions
Fees and margin state

Outputs:
Verified
Partially completed
Failed
Reconciliation required

Verification must use authoritative systems, not the LLM's interpretation.

title: Security
content:
The hardest problem is safely turning intelligence into action.

Principles:
Zero trust
Least privilege
Scoped tools
Typed outputs
Deterministic validation
Sandboxed execution
Approval gates
Rate limits
Kill switches
Full auditability

LLMs must never see private keys, seed phrases, or unrestricted credentials.

title: Prompt Injection Defense
content:
Treat all external content as untrusted data.

Separate system instructions from retrieved content.
Never allow retrieved text to modify security policy.
Restrict tool permissions by user and agent.
Validate tool arguments against schemas.
Apply deterministic policy checks before side effects.
Trace and audit all tool use.

title: RAG and Knowledge
content:
RAG retrieves relevant information before generation.

Pipeline:
Connectors → Processing → Embeddings → Vector Store → Retrieval → Reranking → LLM

Use PostgreSQL for metadata and permissions, pgvector or another vector store for semantic search, and a knowledge graph when entity relationships and dependencies matter.

title: Memory
content:
Memory types:
Session memory = current conversation
User memory = long-term preferences
Task memory = workflow state
Knowledge memory = retrieved information
Audit memory = immutable action history

Live balances, positions, prices, and permissions must always come from authoritative systems rather than conversational memory.

title: Models
content:
LLMs such as GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, and Qwen handle language reasoning and tool selection.

XGBoost and LightGBM handle structured financial prediction.
GARCH handles volatility forecasting.
Monte Carlo handles scenario and risk simulation.
VaR and CVaR estimate portfolio risk.
Isolation Forest and autoencoders detect anomalies.
Embedding models support semantic retrieval.

Agent = decides what to do.
Model = produces a prediction or representation.
Tool = performs an action.

title: Golang and Python
content:
Python is suited to AI, machine learning, retrieval, agent orchestration, FastAPI, data science, and rapid experimentation.

Go is suited to high-performance APIs, concurrency, streaming systems, networking, distributed infrastructure, and latency-sensitive execution services.

A practical polyglot architecture can use Python for intelligence and Go for execution-heavy services.

title: Blockchain
content:
Blockchain is a distributed ledger for transaction ordering, state, ownership, and settlement.

Relevant concepts:
Smart contracts
Wallets
Private keys
Layer 1 and Layer 2 networks
DeFi
Decentralized exchanges
On-chain settlement
Tokenized assets
Oracles
Account abstraction
Session keys
Multisignature and MPC custody

title: AI and Blockchain
content:
AI should never directly control unrestricted private keys.

Preferred flow:
Agent → Policy → Risk → Approval → Signing Service → Smart Contract or Execution Network → Verification

Use scoped session keys, workload identity, signing policies, transaction simulation, and explicit limits on assets, contracts, amounts, duration, and permissions.

title: Financial Platform Architecture
content:
A modern financial intelligence platform can combine:
Market data
Portfolio data
News and research
Risk analytics
Prediction models
Natural-language interaction
Alerts
Execution
Verification

The architecture should move from insight to action while keeping deterministic control over permissions, risk, and financial correctness.

title: Scalability
content:
Scale by separating reasoning, policy, execution, and verification into independently deployable services.

Use stateless APIs behind load balancers, horizontal scaling, Redis caching, event streams, durable workflows, database replication, backpressure, rate limits, and full observability.

title: Infrastructure
content:
Cloud = where the platform runs.
Docker = packages services.
Kubernetes = deploys, restarts, and scales containers.
Load balancer = distributes traffic.
PostgreSQL = persistent state and audits.
Redis = cache and short-lived state.
Kafka or NATS = event transport.
Vault or KMS = secrets and signing-key protection.
OpenTelemetry = traces, metrics, and logs.
CI/CD = automated build, test, security checks, and deployment.

title: Observability
content:
Observability means tracing prompts, agent decisions, tool calls, model usage, latency, cost, errors, retries, policy decisions, and final outcomes.

LangSmith can trace and evaluate agent workflows.
OpenTelemetry can trace the full distributed system.

title: Implementation Approach
content:
Start with one narrow, read-only workflow.

Phase 1:
Receive a user question.
Retrieve trusted data.
Run one agent workflow.
Return a structured, cited answer.

Phase 2:
Generate a typed proposal.
Simulate risk.
Apply policy.
Request approval.

Phase 3:
Execute through a restricted service.
Verify the final state.
Reconcile failures.
Maintain a complete audit trail.

title: Canonical Architecture Principle
content: AI reasons → Policy validates → Execution acts → Verification confirms.

title: How Joz Builds
content:
Joz starts with user intent, defines architecture boundaries, exposes capabilities through tools, adds security and policy controls, implements deterministic execution, verifies outcomes, and improves the system through measurement and feedback.

Joz combines systems thinking, financial architecture, AI orchestration, product judgment, and AI-assisted implementation.

title: Organisational Awareness Layer
content:
The Organisational Awareness Layer transforms fragmented company information into trusted collective intelligence.

Architecture:
Connectors → Processing → Knowledge Layer → ACL-Aware Retrieval → Answering

Connectors ingest data from GitHub, Slack, documentation systems, wikis, ticketing tools, architecture repositories, and other approved enterprise sources.

Processing performs data cleaning, normalization, deduplication, chunking, metadata tagging, sensitivity classification, ownership extraction, recency analysis, and embedding generation.

title: Organisational Knowledge Layer
content:
The Organisational Knowledge Layer can combine:

PostgreSQL for metadata, source references, ACLs, ownership records, and audit events.
pgvector for semantic retrieval.
Keyword indexes for exact-term retrieval.
A knowledge graph for ownership, decisions, relationships, dependencies, systems, teams, and architecture.
Object storage for source documents and large artefacts.

The system should preserve provenance so every answer can cite its source.

title: ACL-Aware Retrieval
content:
Permissions must be enforced before retrieval.

The system first authenticates the user, resolves source-system permissions, applies metadata and ACL filters, retrieves only authorized content, reranks results, and then provides permitted context to the LLM.

Unauthorized information must never enter the LLM context window.

ACLs should be inherited from source systems whenever possible and all retrieval operations should be auditable.

title: Organisational Retrieval Strategy
content:
Use hybrid retrieval:

Metadata filtering
Keyword search
Vector search
Graph traversal
Recency scoring
Source-trust scoring
Ownership weighting

Retrieved results should be ranked by relevance, freshness, authority, and permission validity before they are supplied to the LLM.

title: Sensitive Data Boundaries
content:
Do not ingest or expose highly sensitive sources by default, including:

HR files
Salary information
Performance reviews
Private messages
Secrets
Credentials
Private keys
Unapproved personal data

Data-source admission should be explicit, reviewed, and governed.

title: Organisational Awareness Challenges
content:
The primary challenges are:

ACL enforcement
Ownership inference
Knowledge freshness
Conflicting sources
Source trust
Stale decisions
Entity resolution
Maintaining citations
Preventing unauthorized retrieval
Preserving user trust as organisational knowledge changes

title: Autonomous Execution Layer
content:
The Autonomous Execution Layer transforms organisational awareness into governed action.

Agent loop:
Task → Plan → Retrieve Context → Implement → Verify → Repair → Verify → Pull Request

Agents retrieve ownership information, historical decisions, architecture constraints, coding standards, operational requirements, and relevant documentation before making changes.

title: Autonomous Code Verification
content:
Every autonomous code change should pass:

Unit tests
Integration tests
Type checking
Linting
Security scanning
Dependency checks
Architectural checks
Business-intent validation

If verification fails, the agent repairs the issue and repeats the verification cycle.

title: Execution Guardrails
content:
Autonomous agents must not:

Deploy directly to production
Push directly to the main branch
Access unrestricted secrets
Perform destructive operations without approval
Bypass tests
Bypass policy checks
Merge their own pull requests

Execution should occur in an isolated Docker sandbox or equivalent restricted environment.

Database migrations, security changes, infrastructure changes, production deployments, and code merges require explicit human approval.

title: Multi-Layer Verification Framework
content:
Verification should operate across multiple layers:

Technical Verification = tests, linting, type checking, runtime checks
Security Verification = permissions, policy compliance, vulnerability checks
Architectural Verification = alignment with platform standards and approved patterns
Business Verification = alignment with user intent and business requirements
Human Verification = approval gates for high-risk actions

Verification is a system-level function, not merely an LLM self-check.

title: Organisational Intelligence Integration
content:
The Organisational Awareness Layer is the knowledge foundation for the Autonomous Execution Layer.

Before acting, agents retrieve:
Ownership
Historical decisions
Architecture constraints
Operational standards
Relevant documentation
Security policies
Business requirements

This grounds actions in organisational context rather than relying only on repository contents or model assumptions.

title: Core Trust Principle
content: The largest risk is not code generation itself. The largest risk is inaccurate context, misunderstood business intent, weak verification, and loss of trust in autonomous decision-making.
